text_blocks.text: [[i class="fa-solid fa-list"]][[/i]]Contents
[[a href="#section1"]]The Point at infinity[[/a]]
[[a href="#section2"]]Infinite Limits[[/a]]
Riemann Sphere
The Point at infinity

For some purposes it is convenient to introduce the 
                    [[em]]point at infinity[[/em]], denoted by @@math:28:cbe5bf@@
                    in addition to the points @@math:29:9712b9@@ We must be 
                    careful in doing so, because it can lead to
                    confusion and abuse of the symbol @@math:30:44d1f3@@ However, with care 
                    it can be useful, if we want
                    to be able to talk about infinite 
                    limits and limits at infinity.

In contrast to the real line, to which @@math:31:d9f165@@ and @@math:32:bc5247@@ can be added, we have only one @@math:33:fac19f@@ for @@math:34:04e44b@@ The reason is that @@math:35:6ba4a5@@ has no natural ordering as @@math:36:359d99@@ does. Formally we add a symbol @@math:37:87c188@@ to @@math:38:b45c25@@ to obtain the [[em]]extended complex plane[[/em]], denoted by $\mathbb C^*=\mathbb C \cup \{\infty\}.$ We establish operations with @@math:39:4fceb6@@ by setting @@math:8:b8957f@@ for every @@math:40:f1d5e4@@ and @@math:9:ddf040@@ for all @@math:41:fc9cf7@@ including @@math:42:2790d4@@ However, it is not possible to define
@@math:10:1ac235@@
because there is no consistent algebraic or geometric interpretations for these operations.

By special convention we also define
@@math:43:e1ad33@@
The quotients @@math:44:f183f9@@ and @@math:45:8e3c28@@ are undefined.

The extended complex plane can be mapped onto the
                    surface of a sphere whose south pole corresponds to the origin and whose north
                    pole to the point @@math:46:566cbb@@ All other points of the complex plane can be mapped in
                    a one-to-one fashion to points on the surface of the sphere by using the following
                    construction. Connect the point @@math:47:7f6677@@ in the plane with the north pole using a
                    straight line. This line intersects the sphere at the point @@math:48:fff4bf@@ In this way each
                    point @@math:49:69640d@@ on the complex plane corresponds uniquely to a point @@math:50:267211@@ on the
                    surface of the sphere. This construction is called the [[em]]stereographic projection[[/em]] and is
                    illustrated
                    in the following applet.

In the following applet we can observe the unit sphere whose south pole
                    corresponds to the origin of the @@math:51:df6914@@ plane.
                    Drag the point defined on the @@math:52:75a03c@@ plane, or the sliders,
                    to explore the behaviour of the point @@math:53:480496@@ on the sphere.

The extended complex plane is sometimes referred to as the [[em]]compactified[[/em]] (closed) complex
                    plane. It
                    is often useful to view the complex plane in this way, and knowledge of the construction of the
                    stereographic projection is valuable in certain advanced treatments.
Infinite Limits

Now we can introduce the following limit concepts:

Riemann Sphere

The Point at infinity

For some purposes it is convenient to introduce the point at infinity, denoted by @@math:28:cbe5bf@@ in addition to the points @@math:29:9712b9@@ We must be careful in doing so, because it can lead to confusion and abuse of the symbol @@math:30:44d1f3@@ However, with care it can be useful, if we want to be able to talk about infinite limits and limits at infinity.

In contrast to the real line, to which @@math:31:d9f165@@ and @@math:32:bc5247@@ can be added, we have only one @@math:33:fac19f@@ for @@math:34:04e44b@@ The reason is that @@math:35:6ba4a5@@ has no natural ordering as @@math:36:359d99@@ does. Formally we add a symbol @@math:37:87c188@@ to @@math:38:b45c25@@ to obtain the extended complex plane, denoted by $\mathbb C^*=\mathbb C \cup \{\infty\}.$ We establish operations with @@math:39:4fceb6@@ by setting @@math:8:b8957f@@ for every @@math:40:f1d5e4@@ and @@math:9:ddf040@@ for all @@math:41:fc9cf7@@ including @@math:42:2790d4@@ However, it is not possible to define

because there is no consistent algebraic or geometric interpretations for these operations.

The quotients @@math:44:f183f9@@ and @@math:45:8e3c28@@ are undefined.

The extended complex plane can be mapped onto the surface of a sphere whose south pole corresponds to the origin and whose north pole to the point @@math:46:566cbb@@ All other points of the complex plane can be mapped in a one-to-one fashion to points on the surface of the sphere by using the following construction. Connect the point @@math:47:7f6677@@ in the plane with the north pole using a straight line. This line intersects the sphere at the point @@math:48:fff4bf@@ In this way each point @@math:49:69640d@@ on the complex plane corresponds uniquely to a point @@math:50:267211@@ on the surface of the sphere. This construction is called the stereographic projection and is illustrated in the following applet.

In the following applet we can observe the unit sphere whose south pole corresponds to the origin of the @@math:51:df6914@@ plane. Drag the point defined on the @@math:52:75a03c@@ plane, or the sliders, to explore the behaviour of the point @@math:53:480496@@ on the sphere.

The extended complex plane is sometimes referred to as the compactified (closed) complex plane. It is often useful to view the complex plane in this way, and knowledge of the construction of the stereographic projection is valuable in certain advanced treatments.
